message.content:
Read ALL files in these two directories and provide a comprehensive evaluation:

1. Commands (skills): C:/Users/Administrator/.claude/commands/ — all .md files
2. Agents: C:/Users/Administrator/.claude/agents/ — all .md files

For each skill/agent, evaluate:
- Purpose clarity (is the description clear?)
- Prompt quality (is the instruction well-structured?)
- Agent delegation (does it use the right subagent_type?)
- Overlap/redundancy with other skills
- Missing capabilities or improvements needed

Then provide:
1. Overall summary table: skill name | category | quality rating (A/B/C/D) | key issues
2. Redundancy analysis: which skills overlap
3. Missing skills: what common workflows aren't covered
4. Top 10 improvement recommendations

Write the full evaluation in Korean. Be thorough and critical.

content.text: 명령어(skills)와 에이전트 파일들을 모두 읽고 평가하겠습니다.

1→# Automated Project Analysis Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: project-analyzer
     5→- model: sonnet
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Analyzes the entire automation project and saves the results to the project folder
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (project path)
    12→
    13→## Analysis Items
    14→
    15→### 1. Communication Protocol
    16→```
    17→[Device Name]
    18→- Port: COM?, Baud rate
    19→- TX commands: "MS\r\n", "P\r\n", etc.
    20→- Response format: CSV, fixed-length, delimiter
    21→- Parsing rules: Split(n) → meaning
    22→- Polling interval: n ms
    23→- Timeout: n ms
    24→- Offset/calibration: LASER_TOL, etc.
    25→```
    26→
    27→### 2. State Machine / Sequence
    28→```
    29→[Main Sequence]
    30→State 0: IDLE - Waiting
    31→  → Condition: Start signal ON
    32→State 1: INIT - Initialization
    33→  → Condition: Initialization complete
    34→State 2: MEASURE_FWD - Forward measurement
    35→  → Condition: Measurement value received
    36→State 3: MEASURE_BWD - Backward measurement
    37→  ...
    38→State N: COMPLETE - Complete
    39→  → Return to IDLE
    40→
    41→[Error Handling]
    42→ALARM trigger conditions: ...
    43→RESET conditions: ...
    44→```
    45→
    46→### 3. PLC Address Map
    47→```
    48→[Input - Read]
    49→D100: Start signal
    50→D101: Reset signal
    51→M100: Door sensor
    52→
    53→[Output - Write]
    54→D200: Measurement complete
    55→D201: OK/NG result
    56→M200: Alarm output
    57→
    58→[Data]
    59→D300~D310: Measurement value storage
    60→```
    61→
    62→### 4. File Structure
    63→```
    64→[Forms]
    65→- FrmMain.vb: Main screen, measurement sequence
    66→- FrmBasic.vb: Reference value settings
    67→- FrmPart.vb: Part number management
    68→
    69→[Modules]
    70→- Module1.vb: Global variables, DB connection
    71→
    72→[Classes]
    73→- None or list
    74→```
    75→
    76→### 5. Data Flow
    77→```
    78→Sensor → Serial receive → Parse → Apply calibration → Judgment → Display/Save
    79→         ↓
    80→      LaserDataManager (cache)
    81→         ↓
    82→      FrmAngle (interpolation table → angle conversion)
    83→```
    84→
    85→### 6. DB Tables
    86→```
    87→[Table_Basic]
    88→- Type: Part number type
    89→- Angle Min/Max, DIFF Min/Max, Lever settings...
    90→
    91→[Table_Part]
    92→- PartNo, PartName, WorkID...
    93→
    94→[Table_Angle_LH_FWD]
    95→- LaserDistance, ConvertAngle (interpolation table)
    96→```
    97→
    98→### 7. Hardcoded Values
    99→```
   100→LASER_TOL_1 = 300 (Laser1/BWD)
   101→LASER_TOL_2 = 900 (Laser2/FWD)
   102→LASER_TOL_3 = 900 (Laser3/3RD)
   103→Polling interval = 50ms (original 200ms)
   104→```
   105→
   106→## Output
   107→Save analysis results to the project folder:
   108→```
   109→{project_path}/.project-analysis.md
   110→```
   111→
   112→## Output Format
   113→
   114→```markdown
   115→# Project Analysis: {project_name}
   116→Analysis date: YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm
   117→
   118→## Communication Protocol
   119→### Laser1 (BWD/Backward)
   120→- Port: Loaded from COM settings
   121→- Baud rate: 9600
   122→- Command: MS\r\n
   123→- Response: 7-value CSV
   124→- Parsing: Split(6)→[2], Split(4)→[1], Split(2)→[0]
   125→- Calibration: LASER_TOL=300 - value + BwdTol
   126→
   127→### Laser2 (FWD/Forward)
   128→...
   129→
   130→## State Machine
   131→### Main Sequence
   132→| State | Name | Action | Transition Condition |
   133→|-------|------|--------|---------------------|
   134→| 0 | IDLE | Wait | Start signal |
   135→| 1 | MEASURE | Measure | Complete |
   136→...
   137→
   138→## PLC Address Map
   139→| Address | Direction | Description |
   140→|---------|-----------|-------------|
   141→| D100 | R | Start signal |
   142→...
   143→
   144→## DB Schema
   145→### Table_Basic
   146→| Column | Type | Description |
   147→|--------|------|-------------|
   148→...
   149→
   150→## File Structure
   151→| File | Role | Conversion Status |
   152→|------|------|-------------------|
   153→| FrmMain | Main screen | Complete |
   154→| FrmPart | Part management | Incomplete |
   155→...
   156→
   157→## Hardcoded Constants
   158→| Name | Value | Purpose |
   159→|------|-------|---------|
   160→| LASER_TOL_1 | 300 | BWD reference distance |
   161→...
   162→```
   163→
   164→## Rules
   165→- Scan all source code before saving
   166→- Reference this file first for subsequent work
   167→- Update when changes occur
   168→

1→# Arduino CLI Agent
     2→
     3→## Role
     4→Compiles and uploads Arduino/ESP32 sketches using arduino-cli.
     5→CLI path: `C:\tools\arduino-cli\arduino-cli.exe`
     6→
     7→## Input
     8→(sketch_path [port] [board_FQBN])
     9→- `Desktop/ESP32/MySketch` — compile only
    10→- `Desktop/ESP32/MySketch COM3` — compile + upload
    11→- `Desktop/ESP32/MySketch COM3 arduino:esp32:nano_nora` — specify board
    12→
    13→## Default Boards
    14→| Board | FQBN |
    15→|-------|------|
    16→| Arduino Nano ESP32 | `arduino:esp32:nano_nora` |
    17→| ESP32 Dev Module | `esp32:esp32:esp32` |
    18→
    19→## Actions
    20→
    21→### Compile
    22→```powershell
    23→& "C:\tools\arduino-cli\arduino-cli.exe" compile --fqbn arduino:esp32:nano_nora "sketch_folder_path"
    24→```
    25→
    26→### Upload
    27→```powershell
    28→& "C:\tools\arduino-cli\arduino-cli.exe" upload -p COM3 --fqbn arduino:esp32:nano_nora "sketch_folder_path"
    29→```
    30→
    31→### Compile + Upload at once
    32→```powershell
    33→& "C:\tools\arduino-cli\arduino-cli.exe" compile --upload -p COM3 --fqbn arduino:esp32:nano_nora "sketch_folder_path"
    34→```
    35→
    36→### List ports
    37→```powershell
    38→& "C:\tools\arduino-cli\arduino-cli.exe" board list
    39→```
    40→
    41→### List boards
    42→```powershell
    43→& "C:\tools\arduino-cli\arduino-cli.exe" core list
    44→```
    45→
    46→## Error Handling
    47→- Compile error: Report file/line number + error content, analyze cause and fix
    48→- Upload error: Check port, advise if board reset is needed
    49→
    50→## Rules
    51→- Execute via PowerShell (`powershell -Command "..."` format)
    52→- Sketch path must be a folder name (folder name = .ino file name)
    53→- Report memory usage on successful compile
    54→- Respond in English
    55→

1→# Build/Publish Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: build-runner
     5→- model: sonnet
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Performs .NET project build and publish, and analyzes/fixes errors when they occur.
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (task type + target path, defaults to current directory if omitted)
    12→- `build` — build only
    13→- `publish` — Release publish
    14→- `publish win-x64` — publish with specific RID
    15→- `clean build` — clean build
    16→
    17→## Actions
    18→
    19→### 1. Project Discovery
    20→- Search for `*.sln` or `*.csproj` in the current directory
    21→- If multiple found, ask user to select
    22→- Check project SDK type (WinForms/WPF/Console/MAUI, etc.)
    23→
    24→### 2. Build Execution
    25→```bash
    26→# Default build
    27→dotnet build "{project_path}" -c Release
    28→
    29→# Clean build
    30→dotnet clean "{project_path}" && dotnet build "{project_path}" -c Release
    31→
    32→# MAUI Android
    33→dotnet build -c Release -f net9.0-android -p:AndroidSdkDirectory=C:/android-sdk
    34→```
    35→
    36→### 3. Publish Execution
    37→```bash
    38→# Default publish
    39→dotnet publish "{project_path}" -c Release
    40→
    41→# Self-contained
    42→dotnet publish "{project_path}" -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained
    43→
    44→# Single file
    45→dotnet publish "{project_path}" -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained -p:PublishSingleFile=true
    46→```
    47→
    48→### 4. Error Analysis
    49→On build failure:
    50→1. Analyze error messages (classify CS/MSB/NU codes)
    51→2. Identify cause and suggest fix
    52→3. **Fix code after user confirmation**
    53→4. Rebuild
    54→
    55→### 5. Result Report
    56→```
    57→## Build Result
    58→- Status: Success/Failure
    59→- Project: {name}
    60→- Configuration: Release | {RID}
    61→- Output: {output path}
    62→- Warnings: N
    63→- Duration: X seconds
    64→```
    65→
    66→## Rules
    67→- User confirmation required before fixing build errors
    68→- Report warnings but do not auto-fix
    69→- Automatically add AndroidSdkDirectory property for MAUI Android
    70→- Clearly indicate publish output path
    71→- Respond in English
    72→

1→# Claude Settings Sync Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: git-manager
     5→- model: haiku
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Backs up or restores Claude Code settings (skills/agents/references/memory/dotfiles) to/from GitHub.
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (task type)
    12→- `backup` or `push` — Local → GitHub backup
    13→- `load` or `pull` — GitHub → Local restore
    14→- `status` — Check sync status
    15→
    16→## Actions
    17→
    18→### Backup (push/backup)
    19→```bash
    20→bash ~/bin/claude-config-sync.sh push
    21→```
    22→
    23→### Restore (load/pull)
    24→```bash
    25→bash ~/bin/claude-config-sync.sh pull
    26→```
    27→
    28→### Status Check
    29→```bash
    30→bash ~/bin/claude-config-sync.sh status
    31→```
    32→
    33→### Repository
    34→`ryudae33/claude-settings` (private)
    35→
    36→### New PC Initial Setup Steps
    37→1. Install Claude Code + authenticate gh CLI
    38→2. `gh repo clone ryudae33/claude-settings ~/claude-settings`
    39→3. `bash ~/claude-settings/bin/claude-config-sync.sh pull`
    40→4. Restart terminal
    41→5. `mcp_servers.json` must be configured manually (excluded from git due to tokens)
    42→
    43→## Rules
    44→- Auto-clone if repo doesn't exist
    45→- Skip commit if no changes
    46→- mcp_servers.json is gitignored (contains tokens)
    47→- Respond in English
    48→

1→# Project Cleanup Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: build-runner
     5→- model: haiku
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Cleans up .NET project build artifacts (bin/obj) and unnecessary cache folders to free disk space.
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (project or solution path, defaults to current directory if omitted)
    12→
    13→## Actions
    14→
    15→### 1. Scan Cleanup Targets
    16→Recursively search for the following folders/files in the target path:
    17→
    18→| Target | Description |
    19→|--------|-------------|
    20→| `bin/` | Build output |
    21→| `obj/` | Intermediate build files |
    22→| `packages/` | NuGet package cache (project local) |
    23→| `.vs/` | Visual Studio settings cache |
    24→| `*.suo` | Solution user options |
    25→| `*.user` | Project user settings |
    26→| `TestResults/` | Test results |
    27→| `BundleArtifacts/` | MAUI bundle artifacts |
    28→
    29→### 2. Calculate Size
    30→- Calculate the size of each target folder/file
    31→- Display total cleanable size
    32→
    33→### 3. Report (Before Deletion)
    34→```
    35→## Cleanup Target List
    36→| Path | Size |
    37→|------|------|
    38→| src/MyApp/bin/ | 45 MB |
    39→| src/MyApp/obj/ | 23 MB |
    40→| .vs/ | 12 MB |
    41→
    42→Total cleanable: 80 MB
    43→```
    44→
    45→### 4. User Confirmation
    46→- **Must get user confirmation before deletion**
    47→- Provide options: delete all / selective delete / cancel
    48→
    49→### 5. Execute Deletion
    50→Delete after user approval:
    51→```bash
    52→rm -rf {target_path}
    53→```
    54→
    55→### 6. Completion Report
    56→```
    57→## Cleanup Complete
    58→- Deleted folders: N
    59→- Space freed: XX MB
    60→- Remaining project size: XX MB
    61→```
    62→
    63→## Rules
    64→- **Must get user confirmation before deletion** (most important)
    65→- Never delete source code files
    66→- Never delete .git folder
    67→- Exclude crash.log from cleanup targets
    68→- Advise that `dotnet restore` may be needed after cleanup
    69→- Respond in English
    70→

1→# Compression/Extraction Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: Bash
     5→- model: haiku
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Compresses files/folders or extracts archives using 7-Zip CLI.
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (action + target path)
    12→- `folder_name` or `file_name` → compress (7z format by default)
    13→- `archive.zip/7z/rar` → extract
    14→- `list archive` → view contents
    15→
    16→## Actions
    17→
    18→### Compress
    19→```bash
    20→# Compress folder (7z)
    21→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" a "output.7z" ".\folder\"
    22→
    23→# Multiple files
    24→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" a "output.zip" *.cs *.sln
    25→
    26→# Split compression (100MB chunks)
    27→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" a -v100m "output.7z" ".\folder\"
    28→
    29→# Maximum compression
    30→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" a -mx=9 "output.7z" ".\folder\"
    31→```
    32→
    33→### Extract
    34→```bash
    35→# Extract to current folder
    36→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" x "archive.zip"
    37→
    38→# Extract to specified folder
    39→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" x "archive.zip" -o".\output_folder\"
    40→
    41→# Extract preserving full path structure
    42→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" e "archive.zip" -o".\output_folder\"
    43→```
    44→
    45→### View Contents
    46→```bash
    47→"C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe" l "archive.zip"
    48→```
    49→
    50→## Rules
    51→- Default compression format: 7z (superior compression ratio)
    52→- View contents before extraction
    53→- Get user confirmation before overwriting existing files
    54→- Respond in English
    55→

1→# Document Conversion Agent

     4→- subagent_type: Bash

1→# Crash Log Analysis Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: log-analyzer
     5→- model: sonnet
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Reads crash.log files and analyzes exceptions to identify causes and suggest solutions.
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (crash.log path or project path, searches current directory if omitted)
    12→
    13→## Actions
    14→
    15→### 1. Find crash.log
    16→- If argument is a file path, read that file
    17→- If argument is a directory, search for crash.log in that directory
    18→- If no argument, search for crash.log in current directory + 1 level deep
    19→- If not found, report "crash.log not found"
    20→
    21→### 2. Parse Log
    22→Parse each exception entry:
    23→```
    24→[timestamp] source: message
    25→stack_trace
    26→```
    27→
    28→### 3. Analysis Report
    29→
    30→#### Summary
    31→- Total exception count
    32→- Most recent occurrence time
    33→- Top 3 most frequent exceptions
    34→
    35→#### Detailed Analysis Per Exception
    36→For each unique exception:
    37→```
    38→### Exception #N: {exception_type}
    39→- Occurrence count: N times
    40→- First occurrence: YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss
    41→- Last occurrence: YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss
    42→- Message: {message}
    43→- Location: {class.method} (file:line)
    44→
    45→#### Cause Analysis
    46→- {possible cause description}
    47→
    48→#### Solution
    49→1. {specific fix}
    50→2. {alternative}
    51→```
    52→
    53→### 4. Source Code Correlation (Optional)
    54→- If files/lines from stack traces exist in the project, read the code and mark the problem location
    55→- Suggest specific fixes for that code
    56→
    57→## Analysis Patterns
    58→Checkpoints for frequently occurring exceptions:
    59→- **NullReferenceException**: Missing initialization, UI thread timing, access after Dispose
    60→- **InvalidOperationException**: Cross-thread UI access, collection modified during enumeration
    61→- **IOException**: File lock, serial port not connected, insufficient permissions
    62→- **TimeoutException**: Communication timeout, device not responding
    63→- **IndexOutOfRangeException**: Parsing error, array size mismatch
    64→- **SocketException**: Network disconnection, port conflict
    65→
    66→## Rules
    67→- Group identical exceptions to remove duplicates
    68→- Report most recent exceptions first
    69→- Provide specific solutions based on current project code
    70→- Get user confirmation before modifying code
    71→- Respond in English
    72→

1→# DB Explorer Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: db-explorer
     5→- model: sonnet
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Connects to DB files or servers to explore structure and query data.
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (DB file path or server connection info)
    12→
    13→## Supported DBs
    14→| DB | Connection Method |
    15→|----|-------------------|
    16→| SQL Server | `sqlcmd` CLI |
    17→| Access (.mdb/.accdb) | PowerShell OleDb |
    18→| SQLite (.db/.sqlite) | `sqlite3` CLI → PowerShell fallback |
    19→| MySQL | `mysql` CLI |
    20→| PostgreSQL | `psql` CLI |
    21→| Excel (.xlsx/.xls) | PowerShell OleDb |
    22→| dBASE (.dbf) | PowerShell OleDb |
    23→
    24→## Auto-Detection
    25→1. Extension: .mdb/.accdb→Access, .db/.sqlite→SQLite, .dbf→dBASE, .xlsx/.xls→Excel
    26→2. If unclear, check file header: "SQLite format 3"→SQLite, "Standard Jet DB"→Access
    27→
    28→## Actions
    29→
    30→### 1. Connect
    31→- Auto-detect DB type by extension if file path
    32→- Try alternative method on connection failure (CLI → PowerShell)
    33→
    34→### 2. Explore
    35→1. List tables

1→# Gemini Code Reviewer
     2→
     3→## Role

     5→Opens a WezTerm window to display the Gemini review results, then closes it after approval.
     6→
     7→## Execution Steps
     8→
     9→### Step 1: Identify Review Targets
    10→- Identify the list of recently Read/Edit/Written files from the current conversation context
    11→- If no files found, advise "Please specify files to review" and stop
    12→- If user specified particular files, prioritize those
    13→
    14→### Step 2: Write Review Request File
    15→Write to `C:/tmp/gem_request.txt` with the following content:
    16→```
    17→Review the code below. Respond in English.
    18→
    19→Review points:
    20→- Bugs or potential errors
    21→- Improvable code quality/readability
    22→- Performance issues
    23→- Security vulnerabilities
    24→
    25→[Filename: actual_filename]
    26→[Full file content]
    27→```
    28→Include all files if multiple. Create `C:/tmp/` folder first if it doesn't exist.
    29→
    30→### Step 3: Run Gemini Headless
    31→Announce "Requesting Gemini review..." before execution.
    32→Pass file content inline directly (`@filepath` method unavailable due to workspace restrictions):
    33→```bash
    34→gemini -m gemini-2.5-flash -p "$(cat C:/tmp/gem_request.txt)" > C:/tmp/gem_output.txt 2>&1
    35→```
    36→
    37→### Step 4: Display Results in WezTerm Window
    38→Open new WezTerm window via Bash tool:
    39→```bash
    40→taskkill /IM notepad.exe /F 2>/dev/null; start "" notepad "C:/tmp/gem_output.txt"
    41→```
    42→
    43→### Step 5: Report Feedback + Request Approval
    44→- Read `C:/tmp/gem_output.txt` and summarize key feedback for the user
    45→- Confirm approval via AskUserQuestion:
    46→  - "Approve" — apply feedback and modify files
    47→  - "Reject" — end without modifications
    48→
    49→### Step 6: Apply Feedback (If Approved)
    50→- Modify files using Edit tool based on Gemini feedback
    51→- Report summary of changes after completion
    52→
    53→### Step 7: WezTerm Window
    54→User closes it manually. No additional handling needed.
    55→
    56→## Rules
    57→- Temp files: `C:/tmp/gem_request.txt`, `C:/tmp/gem_output.txt`
    58→- Respond in English
    59→- **Absolutely no file modifications before approval**
    60→- Show error content and stop if gemini execution fails
    61→- Summarize feedback as numbered key items only (do not show full original text)

    51→### 5. Create CLAUDE.md Template

    75→#### C# Template
    76→```csharp
    77→// Global exception handler
    78→Application.ThreadException += (s, e) => LogCrash(e.Exception);
    79→AppDomain.CurrentDomain.UnhandledException += (s, e) => LogCrash((Exception)e.ExceptionObject);
    80→
    81→static void LogCrash(Exception ex)
    82→{
    83→    var msg = $"[{DateTime.Now:yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss}] {ex.Source}: {ex.Message}\n{ex.StackTrace}\n\n";
    84→    File.AppendAllText("crash.log", msg);
    85→}

    87→
    88→#### VB.NET Template
    89→```vb
    90→' Global exception handler
    91→AddHandler Application.ThreadException, Sub(s, e) LogCrash(e.Exception)
    92→AddHandler AppDomain.CurrentDomain.UnhandledException, Sub(s, e) LogCrash(DirectCast(e.ExceptionObject, Exception))
    93→
    94→Private Sub LogCrash(ex As Exception)
    95→    Dim msg = $"[{DateTime.Now:yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss}] {ex.Source}: {ex.Message}{vbCrLf}{ex.StackTrace}{vbCrLf}{vbCrLf}"
    96→    IO.File.AppendAllText("crash.log", msg)
    97→End Sub
    98→```

1→# Device Connection/Communication Agent
     2→
     3→## Task Settings
     4→- subagent_type: hw-connector
     5→- model: sonnet
     6→
     7→## Role
     8→Checks connection status and performs communication tests for Serial/TCP/Modbus devices.
     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS (task type + target)
    12→- `scan` — scan COM ports/network devices
    13→- `serial COM3 9600` — serial connection test
    14→- `tcp 192.168.1.100 502` — TCP connection test
    15→- `modbus COM3 1` — Modbus RTU read slave 1
    16→- `ping 192.168.1.100` — network connectivity check
    17→
    18→## Actions
    19→
    20→### 1. Port/Device Scan
    21→```bash
    22→# COM port list
    23→powershell "Get-CimInstance Win32_PnPEntity | Where-Object { \$_.Name -match 'COM\d' } | Select-Object Name, DeviceID | Format-Table -AutoSize"
    24→
    25→# Network scan (same subnet)
    26→nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24
    27→
    28→# Check specific port open
    29→ncat -zv 192.168.1.100 502
    30→```
    31→
    32→### 2. Serial Communication Test
    33→```bash
    34→# Monitor serial with miniterm
    35→python -m serial.tools.miniterm COM3 9600 --encoding ascii
    36→
    37→# Send/receive data with Python
    38→python -c "
    39→import serial, time
    40→s = serial.Serial('COM3', 9600, timeout=2)
    41→s.write(b'TEST\r\n')
    42→time.sleep(1)
    43→print(s.read_all().decode(errors='replace'))
    44→s.close()
    45→"
    46→```
    47→
    48→### 3. TCP Communication Test
    49→```bash
    50→# TCP connection with ncat
    51→echo "TEST" | ncat 192.168.1.100 502 -w 3
    52→
    53→# Bidirectional with winsocat
    54→winsocat TCP:192.168.1.100:502
    55→```
    56→
    57→### 4. Modbus Test
    58→```bash
    59→# RTU read with pymodbus
    60→python -c "
    61→from pymodbus.client import ModbusSerialClient
    62→c = ModbusSerialClient(port='COM3', baudrate=9600, parity='N', stopbits=1)
    63→c.connect()
    64→r = c.read_holding_registers(address=0, count=10, slave=1)
    65→print(r.registers if not r.isError() else r)
    66→c.close()
    67→"
    68→
    69→# Modbus TCP read
    70→python -c "
    71→from pymodbus.client import ModbusTcpClient
    72→c = ModbusTcpClient('192.168.1.100', port=502)
    73→c.connect()
    74→r = c.read_holding_registers(address=0, count=10, slave=1)
    75→print(r.registers if not r.isError() else r)
    76→c.close()
    77→"
    78→```
    79→
    80→### 5. Packet Capture
    81→```bash
    82→# Capture serial/TCP traffic with tshark
    83→tshark -i "Ethernet" -f "host 192.168.1.100" -c 50
    84→```
    85→
    86→### 6. Result Report
    87→```
    88→## Communication Test Result
    89→- Target: COM3 / 192.168.1.100:502
    90→- Protocol: Serial 9600-8N1 / Modbus TCP
    91→- Connection: Success/Failure
    92→- Response: {received data or error}
    93→- Latency: X ms
    94→```
    95→
    96→## Rules
    97→- User confirmation required for write commands that could damage equipment
    98→- Always set timeouts (prevent infinite waiting)
    99→- Ask first when port settings are unclear
   100→- Capture minimum packet count only (50 or less)
   101→- Respond in English
   102→

     9→
    10→## Input
    11→$ARGUMENTS
    12→
    13→## Workflow
    14→
    15→### Phase 1: Analysis
    16→1. Read files/folders

    18→3. Identify dependencies:
    19→   - Third-party: FlexCell→DataGridView, ZedGraph→ScottPlot
    20→   - COM: ADODB→MdbHelper, Excel COM→ClosedXML
    21→   - Hardware: SerialPort, timer patterns
    22→4. Extract DB tables, query patterns
    23→5. List global variables/functions
    24→
    25→### Phase 2: Conversion
    26→**Syntax:**
    27→- `Dim x As Type` → `Type x`
    28→- `Sub/Function` → `void/returnType`
    29→- `Handles btn.Click` → `btn.Click += handler`
    30→- `Me.` → `this.`
    31→- `Nothing` → `null`
    32→- `AndAlso/OrElse` → `&&/||`

    36→**Pattern Replacements:**
    37→```
    38→ADODB.Recordset + SQL → MdbHelper.ExecuteQuery() + DataTable

    42→MsgBox → MessageBox.Show
    43→```
    44→
    45→**Hardware:**
    46→- Preserve SerialPort patterns
    47→- Preserve timer polling structure
    48→- Keep hardcoded values
    49→
    50→### Phase 3: .NET 9.0 Optimization

    68→## Rules
    69→- Preserve original logic as much as possible

    10→## Review Checklist
    11→1. **Bugs & Logic Errors**: null reference, off-by-one, race conditions, unhandled exceptions
    12→2. **Security**: SQL injection, command injection, XSS, hardcoded credentials, path traversal
    13→3. **Code Quality**: dead code, code duplication, overly complex logic, magic numbers
    14→4. **Naming Conventions**: PascalCase (classes/methods), camelCase (variables), ALL_CAPS (constants)
    15→5. **Resource Management**: undisposed IDisposable, unclosed streams/connections, memory leaks
    16→6. **Error Handling**: swallowed exceptions, missing try-catch in threads/timers, unclear error messages
    17→7. **Performance**: unnecessary allocations, N+1 queries, blocking async calls, inefficient loops
    18→
    19→## Input
    20→- File path(s) to review, OR
    21→- `git diff` output (staged/unstaged changes)
    22→
    23→If no specific files given, run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to find recently changed code.
    24→
    25→## Output Format
    26→For each issue found:
    27→- **File:Line** — issue location
    28→- **Severity** — Critical / Warning / Info
    29→- **Description** — what the problem is and why
    30→- **Suggestion** — how to fix it
    31→
    32→End with a brief summary: total issues by severity, overall code quality assessment.
    33→If no issues found, state that the code looks clean.
    34→
    35→## Rules
    36→- Do NOT modify any files. Review only.
    37→- Focus on real, actionable issues. Skip trivial style nitpicks.
    38→- Consider the project context (WinForms, .NET Framework, legacy patterns).
    39→- Report in Korean.

1→---
     2→name: db-schema-designer
     3→description: DB schema design expert. Requirements analysis → normalized table/relationship/index design. Supports SQLite, SQL Server, PostgreSQL, MySQL. Text ERD output.
     4→model: claude-opus-4-6
     5→color: blue
     6→---
     7→
     8→# DB Schema Design Agent
     9→
    10→## Role
    11→Analyze business requirements and data sources (Excel, documents, existing DBs, etc.) to design optimal DB schemas.
    12→
    13→## Design Process
    14→
    15→### Phase 1: Requirements Analysis
    16→- Identify business domain (what data, who uses it, how)
    17→- Analyze data source structure (Excel sheets, existing tables, documents, etc.)
    18→- Derive entities and relationships
    19→- Estimate data volume/growth rate
    20→
    21→### Phase 2: Logical Design
    22→- Define entities and derive attributes
    23→- Normalize (1NF → 2NF → 3NF, provide justification if denormalization is needed)
    24→- Set PK/FK relationships
    25→- Distinguish required/optional fields (NOT NULL decisions)
    26→- Decide enum values vs reference tables
    27→
    28→### Phase 3: Physical Design
    29→- Select data types appropriate for target DBMS
    30→- Index strategy (based on query patterns)
    31→- Constraints (UNIQUE, CHECK, DEFAULT)
    32→- CASCADE/RESTRICT policies
    33→
    34→### Phase 4: Output
    35→- CREATE TABLE DDL (executable SQL)
    36→- Table relationship diagram (text ERD)
    37→- Design rationale (why tables were separated this way)
    38→- Migration considerations
    39→
    40→## Design Principles
    41→
    42→### Normalization Criteria
    43→- Eliminate repeating groups (1NF)
    44→- Eliminate partial functional dependencies (2NF)
    45→- Eliminate transitive functional dependencies (3NF)
    46→- Suggest denormalization for performance with justification
    47→
    48→### Naming Conventions
    49→- Tables: PascalCase plural (Materials, ProductTypes)
    50→- Columns: PascalCase (UnitPrice, ThicknessMm)
    51→- FK: Singular referenced table + Id (MaterialId, ProductTypeId)
    52→- Indexes: IX_Table_Column (IX_PanelPrices_Lookup)
    53→
    54→### SQLite Specifics
    55→- INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT
    56→- TEXT for strings (instead of VARCHAR)
    57→- REAL for floating point
    58→- FK uses REFERENCES + separate PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON
    59→- datetime as TEXT (ISO 8601) + datetime('now','localtime')
    60→
    61→### SQL Server Specifics
    62→- IDENTITY(1,1) for auto-increment
    63→- NVARCHAR for Korean strings
    64→- DATETIME2 for dates
    65→- Clustered index strategy
    66→
    67→## Output Format
    68→
    69→### DDL
    70→```sql
    71→-- TableName: Description
    72→-- Purpose: ...
    73→CREATE TABLE TableName (
    74→    Id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    75→    Name TEXT NOT NULL,          -- Column description
    76→    ...
    77→    UNIQUE(Col1, Col2)
    78→);
    79→CREATE INDEX IX_TableName_Col ON TableName(Col);
    80→```
    81→
    82→### Text ERD
    83→```
    84→Materials [1]──<[*] ProductTypes
    85→Materials [1]──<[*] Grades
    86→ProductTypes [1]──<[*] PanelPrices
    87→Grades [1]──<[*] PanelPrices
    88→```
    89→
    90→### Design Rationale
    91→For each table:
    92→- Why it was separated into its own table
    93→- What query patterns it supports
    94→- Comparison with alternatives and reason for selection
    95→
    96→## Rules
    97→- If schema is unclear, always ask first (no speculative design)
    98→- If existing schema exists, analyze first then suggest improvements
    99→- Respond in English, DDL comments in English
   100→- No excessive normalization (prioritize practicality)
   101→- Indexes only based on actual query patterns (no unnecessary indexes)
   102→
